concepts.title: The Rat-trap is a short story by H.G. Wells
concepts.description: The story revolves around a rat-trap that is used to capture wild rats in a London slum. The story highlights the struggle between the rat-trap and the rats, symbolizing the struggle between the oppressors and the oppressed.

concepts.title: The story is a satire
concepts.description: The story is a satire that criticizes the social conditions of London slums during the Industrial Revolution. It highlights the poverty, ignorance, and disease that plagued these areas.

concepts.title: Symbolism of the rat-trap
concepts.description: The rat-trap is a symbol of the oppressive system that traps the poor and the oppressed. The rats represent the working class who are exploited and oppressed by the capitalist system.

concepts.title: To remember the setting of the story
concepts.description: Mnemonic: 'Squalid Slums - Poverty, Ignorance, Disease' (SSPID). This helps to remember the setting of the story and the social conditions of London slums during the Industrial Revolution.

concepts.title: Misinterpretation of the rat-trap
concepts.description: Students often misinterpret the rat-trap as a symbol of the working class's struggle against the oppressors. However, the rat-trap actually represents the oppressive system that traps the poor and the oppressed.

concepts.title: Key themes of the story
concepts.description: The key themes of the story are: oppression, class struggle, satire, social commentary, and the struggle between the haves and the have-nots.

concepts.title: Author's purpose
concepts.description: The author, H.G. Wells, wrote the story to satirize the social conditions of London slums during the Industrial Revolution and to highlight the struggles of the working class.

concepts.title: Characterization of the rats
concepts.description: The rats in the story are characterized as being fierce, cunning, and determined to survive. This highlights the resourcefulness and resilience of the working class in the face of oppression.
